28: me phobeisthe apo ton apokteinonton to soma — do not fear those who kill the body — the first me phobos is addressed to the fear of persecutors; the second phobeisthe de mallon (rather fear) addresses the appropriate fear of God. The contrast is not between two fears of the same kind but between the fear that produces obedience to God and the paralysis produced by fear of humans. ton de psychen me dynamenon apokteinai — those who cannot kill the soul — soul (psyche) as the seat of personal existence that physical death does not terminate.
1: kai proskalesamenos tous dōdeka mathētas — calling to himself the twelve disciples (proskaleomai aorist middle: summoning to oneself; the middle voice emphasizes the personal initiative of Jesus) — edōken autois exousian (gave them authority — exousia, the same word as Matthew 7:29; Matthew 9:6,8): the twelve receive delegated authority, not independent power. Pneumatōn akathartōn (over unclean spirits) and pasan noson kai pasan malakian (every disease and every weakness): the authority given mirrors Jesus's own practice in Matthew 9:35.
2: tōn de dōdeka apostolōn ta onomata — the names of the twelve apostles (apostolos, sent one, from apostellō, to send off with a commission) — this is Matthew's first and only explicit use of the title apostolos for the twelve (cf. Mark 6:30; Luke uses it frequently). The title defines them by their function: they are sent ones, commissioned agents. The list begins with prōtos Simōn ho legomenos Petros (first, Simon who is called Peter) — prōtos marks Peter's precedence in the list, not necessarily in rank.
3: Philippos kai Bartholomaios — the names continue; Maththaios ho telōnēs — Matthew the tax collector: uniquely in Matthew's list, Matthew is identified by his former occupation (the other Gospels simply list his name), a mark of the self-identification of a Gospel written by the same person who was called in Matthew 9:9. The profession is both a confession and a commendation of grace: a tax collector among the sent ones.
4: Simōn ho Kananaios kai Ioudas ho Iskariōtēs — Simon the Zealot (Kananaios from Aramaic qan'an, zealous, probably referring to the Zealot movement or simply to intense piety) and Judas Iscariot — ho kai paradous auton (the one who also betrayed him): Judas is introduced with the shadow of betrayal already attached. The list ends where the passion narrative will end. Iskariōtēs: possibly "man of Kerioth" (a Judean town) or from Aramaic seqarya (assassin), the etymology uncertain.
5: eis hodon ethnōn mē apelthēte kai polin Samaritōn mē eiselthēte — do not go on a road of Gentiles and do not enter a Samaritan city — the restriction is geographical but carries covenantal significance: the first mission is concentrated on Israel, not as an ethnic preference but as the covenant-priority of fulfilling the promises to the lost sheep of the house of Israel (Matthew 10:6) before the universal mission (Matthew 28:19). The restriction is temporary; it is lifted at the resurrection.
6: ta probata ta apolōlota oikou Israēl — the lost sheep of the house of Israel (apolōlota perfect passive participle of apollymi: the sheep that have been lost, currently in the state of lostness) — the same compassion-image as Matthew 9:36 (sheep without a shepherd) but with the perfect tense adding the weight of completed lostness: they are not merely wandering but definitively lost. The phrase echoes Ezek 34's indictment of Israel's shepherds who let the flock be scattered.
7: ēggiken hē basileia tōn ouranōn — the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near (ēggiken perfect of engizō: has come near and remains near, the perfect combining completed approach with present proximity) — the identical proclamation as John the Baptist (Matthew 3:2) and Jesus himself (Matthew 4:17): the twelve proclaim the same message as their sender. Poreuomenoi de kēryssete legontes — going, proclaim saying: the proclamation accompanies movement, not a fixed location.
8: astheountas therapeute, nekrous egeirete, leprous katharizete, daimonia ekballete — heal the sick, raise the dead, cleanse lepers, drive out demons — four aorist imperatives of equal weight; the raising of the dead is listed without emphasis as one item in the series. Dōrean elabete dōrean dote — freely (dōrean, as a gift, gratis, without price: the adverbial accusative of dōron) you received; freely give: the disciples received their authority and gifts without payment to Jesus; they are to extend the same terms. The sentence forbids the commercialization of the miraculous.
9: mē ktēsēsthe chryson mēde argyron mēde chalkon eis tas zōnas hymōn — do not acquire (ktaomai aorist middle subjunctive: to acquire for oneself, to make one's own possession) gold or silver or copper in your belts — the descending order (gold → silver → copper) covers all monetary value. The belt (zōnē) was used as a money-belt in ancient travel. The prohibition is not against having money but against making the mission financially self-providing; dependence on the generosity of those receiving the gospel was itself part of the proclamation.
10: mē pēran eis hodon mēde dyo chitōnas mēde hypodēmata mēde rhabdon — no travel bag for the road nor two tunics nor sandals nor a staff — the list enumerates the standard equipment of an ancient traveler; the disciples are sent without the normal self-sufficiency of travel. Axios gar ho ergatēs tēs trophēs autou — for the worker is worthy of his food (axios, worthy, deserving; trophē, nourishment): the theological principle underlying the prohibition. The community receiving the proclamation owes provision to those who proclaim.
11: exetasate tis en autē axios estin — inquire who in it is worthy (exetazō: to examine thoroughly, investigate; not a casual inquiry but a deliberate assessment of receptivity and hospitality) — axios (worthy): the same word as Matthew 10:10; the worthiness of the worker and the worthiness of the host are parallel. Kakeikei meinate heōs an exelthēte — and there remain until you leave: the instruction against house-hopping preserves the integrity of the mission by not treating hospitality as a ladder of social advancement.
12: aspasasthe autēn — greet it (the household, using aspasasthe aorist imperative of aspazomai: a formal, extended greeting — not a casual wave but the covenant-greeting of shalom) — in Jewish practice, a spoken blessing upon entering a house was a form of pronouncing peace. The greeting carries theological weight: peace (eirēnē) is not just courtesy but a blessing that may or may not "rest" on the household depending on its receptivity (Matthew 10:13).
13: ean men ē hē oikia axia elthatō hē eirēnē hymōn ep autēn — if the house is worthy, let your peace come upon it (elthatō aorist imperative: let it come) — ean de mē ē axia hē eirēnē hymōn pros hymas epistraphētō (if it is not worthy, let your peace return to you — epistraphētō aorist passive imperative of strephō): peace is not lost when rejected; it returns to the one who offered it. The agents of peace are not diminished by its rejection, but the household forfeits what was offered.
14: ektinaxtate ton koniorton tōn podōn hymōn — shake the dust off your feet (ektinassō aorist imperative: to shake out thoroughly) — a gesture of dissociation: by shaking off the town's dust, the disciples symbolically return the town's guilt to itself. In Jewish practice, Jews shook the dust of Gentile lands from their feet when re-entering Israel; here the gesture is applied to Israelite towns that reject the kingdom-proclamation, treating their refusal as equivalent to Gentile rejection.
15: anektoteron estai Sodomois kai Gomorrois en hēmera kriseōs — it will be more bearable for Sodom and Gomorrah on the day of judgment (anektoteros, comparative of anektos: bearable, tolerable) — the comparative establishes gradations of judgment: Sodom and Gomorrah, destroyed for notorious wickedness, face a lighter judgment than the towns of Israel that reject the kingdom-messenger. The logic: responsibility scales with proximity to the gospel; greater revelation produces greater accountability.
16: idou egō apostellō hymas hōs probata en mesō lykōn — behold I send you as sheep in the midst of wolves — apostellō (to send on a commission, the verb from which apostolos comes): the sending is Jesus's act. Phronimoi hōs hoi opheis kai akeraioi hōs hai peristerai — shrewd as snakes and innocent as doves: phronimos (practically wise, discerning — the same word as the wise builder in Matthew 7:24) and akeraios (unmixed, pure, without adulteration: used of unalloyed metal and pure wine). The combination is not cunning-plus-innocence but wisdom-that-does-not-compromise.
17: paradōsousin hymas eis synedria — they will hand you over to councils (synedria, plural: local councils with judicial authority, smaller than the Jerusalem Sanhedrin) — kai en tais synagōgais autōn mastigōsousin hymas (and in their synagogues they will flog you: mastigoō, to flog with a whip, the formal punishment of the synagogue court — up to 39 lashes according to Deuteronomy 25:3 as interpreted in the Mishnah). The persecution comes from within the covenant community, not only from outsiders.
18: kai epi hēgemonas de kai basileis achthēsesthe heneken emou — you will be brought before governors and kings because of me (hēgemōn, governor/prefect like Pilate; basileus, king like Herod) — heneken emou (because of me, on account of me): the persecution is specifically due to association with Jesus, not incidental political trouble. Eis martyrion autois kai tois ethnesin — as a testimony to them and to the Gentiles: the hostile tribunal becomes a platform for witness; suffering produces proclamation.
19: mē merimnēsēte pōs ē ti lalēsēte — do not be anxious how or what you will speak (merimnēsēte aorist subjunctive: do not take up this anxiety) — the double question (pōs, how; ti, what) covers both manner and content of speech under pressure. Dothēsetai gar hymin en ekeinē tē hōra ti lalēsēte — for it will be given to you in that hour what to say (dothēsetai future passive divine): the provision is eschatological-moment specific, not a general license to avoid preparation.
20: ou gar hymeis este hoi lalountes alla to pneuma tou patros hymōn — for it is not you who speak but the Spirit of your Father (pneuma tou patros hymōn, the Spirit of your Father — Matthew's distinctive way of identifying the Spirit, here as the Spirit who belongs to and comes from the Father) — the speaking is genuinely human speech through a genuinely divine agency; both the human subject (hymeis) and the divine subject (to pneuma) are named, not one replacing the other. To laloun en hymin — speaking in/through you.
21: paradōsei de adelphos adelphon eis thanaton kai patēr teknon — brother will betray brother to death and a father his child — paradōsei future of paradidōmi (to hand over, deliver up — the same verb used of Judas betraying Jesus; Matthew 10:4; Matthew 26:16,21): the vocabulary of betrayal applied to family relationships. Epanastēsontai tekna epi goneis — children will rise up against parents (epanistēmi, to rise up against, revolt): the language of insurrection applied to the family structure. This Micah 7:6 echo (cf. Matthew 10:35-36) anticipates the family-division saying.
22: kai esesthe misoumenoi hypo pantōn dia to onoma mou — you will be hated by all on account of my name (misoumenoi present passive participle: ongoing, continuous hatred) — dia to onoma mou (on account of my name): the hatred is not incidental but specifically motivated by association with Jesus. Ho de hypomoinas eis telos sōthēsetai — the one enduring to the end will be saved (hypomeno, to remain under, endure under pressure — not passive resignation but active continuance under weight). Eis telos (to the end): the full duration, wherever the end comes.
23: hotan de diōkōsin hymas en tē polei tautē, pheugete eis tēn heteran — when they persecute you in this city, flee to another (pheugete present imperative: continue fleeing as a practice) — ou mē telesēte tas poleis tou Israēl heōs an elthē ho huios tou anthrōpou — you will not finish the cities of Israel before the Son of Man comes (telesēte aorist subjunctive of teleō, to complete; the double negative ou mē for emphatic futurity). The saying's precise reference to "the coming of the Son of Man" is debated (the resurrection, Pentecost, the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE, or the parousia); the urgency is unambiguous.
24: ouk estin mathētēs hyper ton didaskalon — a student is not above the teacher — the proverb establishes the basic order: disciple/teacher, servant/master. Hyper (above, beyond): the comparative locates the disciple permanently below the teacher in standing. The saying grounds the expectation of persecution: if the Teacher was opposed, the disciple should expect no better treatment. The logic is not fatalistic but orienting.
25: arketon tō mathētē hina genētai hōs ho didaskalos autou — it is enough for the student to become like his teacher (arketon, sufficient, from arkeō: the same word as 6:34 for the day's sufficient trouble) — ei ton oikodespotēn Beelzeboul ekalesian — if they called the master of the house Beelzebul (Beelzeboul: probably from Hebrew Ba'al Zebul, "lord of the exalted house," though the LXX's Baalzebub = "lord of the flies" is also a possible background): they have already called Jesus a demonic ally (Matthew 9:34; Matthew 12:24); the household-members face the same charge.
26: mē oun phobetheite autous — therefore do not fear them (phobeō aorist passive subjunctive: do not be made to fear) — the prohibition rests on the certainty of disclosure: ouden gar estin kekalymmenon ho ouk apokalypsthesetai (nothing is covered that will not be uncovered — apokalyptō future passive divine: God will uncover). Kai krupton ho ou gnōsthēsetai — and nothing hidden that will not be known: what is done and said in secret, including the disciples' private instruction, will be made public in God's time.
27: ho legō hymin en tē skotia eipate en tō phōti — what I tell you in the darkness, speak in the light — en tē skotia (in the darkness): private, intimate instruction. En tō phōti (in the light): public proclamation. The disciples receive teaching in the private darkness of discipleship-with-Jesus and are to broadcast it publicly. Kai ho eis to ous akouete kērysate epi tōn dōmatōn — and what you hear whispered in the ear, proclaim from the rooftops: from intimate whisper to rooftop announcement — the instruction moves outward and upward in scale.
29: ouchi dyo strouthia assariou pōleitai — are not two sparrows sold for an assarion (assarion, the smallest Roman copper coin, worth about 1/16 of a denarius) — the sparrow (strouthion) is the cheapest, most disposable bird in the marketplace. Kai hen ex autōn ou peseitai epi tēn gēn aneu tou patros hymōn — and not one of them will fall to the ground apart from (aneu) your Father: aneu (without, apart from) — not "without the Father's permission" but without the Father's presence and knowledge. Divine providence extends to the individual fall of the most commercially negligible creature.
30: hymon de kai hai triches tēs kephalēs pasai ērithmēmenai eisin — even the hairs of your head are all numbered (ērithmēmenai eisin perfect passive periphrastic: stand numbered, remain in a numbered state) — the perfect participle marks the numbering as a completed and ongoing state, not a one-time count; God's knowledge of the disciples is current and complete. The contrast with the sparrow (which is commercially counted at two per assarion) is that the disciples are not priced at all by weight of hair but individually known to the Father.
31: mē oun phobeisthe pollōn strouthiōn diapherete hymeis — do not fear therefore; you differ in value from many sparrows (diapherō, to carry through, to differ in worth; used for surpassing value as in Matthew 6:26) — the argument is explicit: if the Father's providential knowledge extends to sparrows, which are worth less than the disciples, it certainly extends to the disciples. The prohibition on fear (mē phobeisthe) is grounded in the asymmetric valuation: fear is irrational given the Father's specific knowledge of each disciple.
32: pas oun hostis homologēsei en emoi emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn — everyone who acknowledges me before people (homologeō, to speak the same thing, to confess, to acknowledge openly: the public matching of one's speech to the reality of Jesus) — en emoi (in me, using the instrumental en): to confess in Christ is not merely to speak about him but to speak from within the relationship. Homologēsō kagō en autō emprosthen tou patros mou — I will also acknowledge him before my Father: the correspondence is exact — human public acknowledgment answered by Jesus's acknowledgment before the Father.
33: hostis d'an arnēsētai me emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn — whoever denies me before people (arneomai: to deny, disown, say no to — the same verb Peter will use in Matthew 26:70,72,74) — arnēsomai kagō auton emprosthen tou patros mou (I will also deny him before my Father): the symmetry of Matthew 10:32-33 is stark: public acknowledgment → acknowledged by Jesus; public denial → denied by Jesus. The judgment is not punitive addition but the logical extension of the relationship one has chosen to deny.
34: mē nomisēte hoti ēlthon balein eirēnēn epi tēn gēn — do not think that I came to throw peace on the earth (balein, to throw, cast — an active, energetic verb: not "to bring peace" gently but to throw it; nomisēte, aorist subjunctive of nomizō, to suppose, think as a matter of convention) — ouk ēlthon balein eirēnēn alla machairan (I came not to throw peace but a sword — machaira, the short single-bladed cutting sword used in daily life and execution). The sword is not Jesus's goal but the effect of his presence: the truth he brings forces decisions that divide.
35: dichazō — to divide, set at variance (ēlthon dichazai anthrōpon kata tou patros autou, I came to set a man against his father) — the citation of Micah 7:6 frames the family-division as something declared in Scripture, not a new social disruption invented by Jesus. Kai thygatera kata tēs mētros autēs (daughter against her mother) kai nymphēn kata tēs pentheras autēs (daughter-in-law against mother-in-law): the three pairings are intergenerational, covering the household structure.
36: kai echthroi tou anthrōpou hoi oikiakoi autou — a person's enemies are the members of his household (oikiakoi, household members, those of the same house) — the direct quotation of Micah 7:6 LXX. The verse identifies the source of the opposition named in Matthew 10:21-22 (brother against brother, children against parents): those who oppose the disciple because of Jesus are closest in relationship. This is not a sociological observation but an eschatological pressure: the kingdom's arrival produces judgment-level divisions within families.
37: ho philōn patera ē mētera hyper eme ouk estin mou axios — whoever loves (phileō, to love by natural affection) father or mother more than me is not worthy of me (axios) — phileō is the word for natural, instinctive family love; the verse does not prohibit love of family but addresses priority when the two loyalties conflict. Hyper eme (more than me, beyond me): the comparison is explicit. The same structure governs the son/daughter clause: ho philōn huion ē thygatera hyper eme ouk estin mou axios.
38: kai hos ou lambanei ton stauron autou kai akolouthei opisō mou — whoever does not take up his cross and follow after me (lambanei ton stauron, take the cross — lambanō present: ongoing taking up) — the cross as the instrument of execution by crucifixion: a shocking image spoken before the passion narrative, when a person carrying their cross was a condemned person on their way to execution. Opisō mou (after me, behind me): the disciple follows in the direction Jesus has walked, including toward the cross. Ouk estin mou axios — is not worthy of me.
39: ho heurōn tēn psychēn autou apolesei autēn — the one who finds his life will lose it (heurōn aorist participle; apolesei future: will destroy/lose it — apollymi, the word for final ruin as well as ordinary losing) — kai ho apolesas tēn psychēn autou heneken emou heurēsei autēn (and whoever loses his life for my sake will find it): the paradox of values operates on psychē (life, soul), a word spanning physical life, personal identity, and the whole self. The one who protects self-life loses it; the one who surrenders it for Jesus finds the real thing.
40: ho dechomenos hymas eme dechetai — the one who receives you receives me (dechomai: to receive, welcome as a guest) — the chain of identification: receiving the disciple = receiving Jesus = receiving the one who sent Jesus (ton aposteilanta me, the one who sent me: God). The representative logic makes the disciples's reception or rejection a reception or rejection of Jesus himself, and through him of the Father. The same chain appears in reverse in Matthew 10:33 (denying the disciple ↔ denying Jesus).
41: ho dechomenos prophētēn eis onoma prophētou misthon prophētou lēmpsetai — whoever receives a prophet in the name of a prophet will receive a prophet's reward (eis onoma prophētou, in the name of a prophet: receiving someone specifically because they are a prophet, in recognition of their prophetic identity) — misthon prophētou (a prophet's reward): the same misthos (wage, reward owed) as in 5:12 and Matthew 6:1-18. Receiving the prophet because he is a prophet associates the receiver with the prophet's mission and its reward.
42: potērion psychrou hydatos monon — a cup of cold water only (psychros, cold — cold water is more costly in desert climates, unlike warm water which required no effort to keep warm) — tōn mikrōn toutōn (of these little ones — mikros, small, little: the disciples viewed from the world's perspective are small; the diminutive is not derogatory but realistic about their social standing). Ou mē apolese ton misthon autou — will certainly not lose his reward (ou mē + aorist subjunctive: the strongest possible negation of futurity). Even the smallest act of hospitality toward the disciples of Jesus has covenantal weight.
